# MP6: Kafka Streaming Pipeline for Social Engagement Data

**Course:** CS 498 Cloud Computing Applications — University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, MCS-DS

## Overview

This assignment demonstrates why synchronous, request-based architectures break down under bursty traffic, then builds a resilient streaming pipeline using Apache Kafka on AWS MSK (Managed Streaming for Kafka) to handle the same load reliably.

**Section 1 — the baseline:** a simple synchronous Flask consumer running on a single EC2 instance is load-tested with traffic generated by an AWS Lambda function. Under a traffic spike, the consumer's CPU and memory get overwhelmed and it stops responding — showing the limitation of a synchronous, single-instance design.

**Section 2 — the resilient version:** the same kind of traffic is instead published to a Kafka topic on AWS MSK. A Flask-based producer publishes events to the topic; a separate consumer reads from that topic independently and writes each event into HBase (running on Amazon EMR) for storage. Decoupling ingestion (producer) from processing (consumer) through Kafka means the producer never blocks waiting on the consumer, and adding more consumers/partitions improves throughput and reduces consumer lag under the same spiky traffic.

Monitoring (Prometheus + Grafana) tracks Kafka broker and consumer metrics to visualize consumer lag and confirm the improvement.

## What's in this repo

| File | What it does |
|---|---|
| `section1_producer.py` | AWS Lambda function that simulates load against the Section 1 consumer at increasing rates, checking consumer health between stages. |
| `section1_consumer.py` | Synchronous Flask consumer template for the Section 1 baseline (`/consume`, `/health` endpoints). |
| `section2_producer_OG.py` | Original blank template for the Kafka producer, before implementation. |
| `section2_producer.py` | Completed Kafka producer: Flask app that authenticates to AWS MSK via IAM and publishes engagement events to a Kafka topic (`/produce`, `/health` endpoints). |
| `section2_consumer-OG.py` | Original blank template for the Kafka consumer, before implementation. |
| `section2_consumer.py` | Completed Kafka consumer: reads from the Kafka topic and writes each event into HBase (Amazon EMR) for storage, with a `/health` endpoint for readiness checks. |
| `traffic_generator_lambda.py` | AWS Lambda function that loads sample engagement data from S3 and sends it to the producer at two rates — stable vs. spiked — to simulate realistic and bursty traffic. |
| `prometheus.yml` / `targets.json` | Prometheus monitoring configuration for scraping Kafka broker and consumer metrics. |
| `engagement_data.csv` | Sample social media engagement data (post ID, user ID, likes, comments, timestamp) used to simulate realistic traffic. |
| `submit.py` | The course's automated grading submission script. **Redacted** before publishing here — see note below. |

The `_OG` (original) files are kept alongside the completed versions to show the before/after — what was template versus what was implemented.

## Architecture (high level)

```
Traffic Generator (Lambda, reads engagement_data.csv from S3)
        │
        ▼
   Producer (Flask)
        │
        ├── Section 1: direct synchronous call to a single EC2 consumer
        │              → overwhelmed and crashes under a traffic spike
        │
        └── Section 2: publishes to a Kafka topic on AWS MSK
                              │
                              ▼
                   Kafka Consumer(s) (Flask + KafkaConsumer)
                              │
                              ▼
                    HBase on Amazon EMR (storage)

Prometheus + Grafana monitor consumer lag throughout
```

## Note on redacted content

The original `submit.py` contained a real grading secret token and live EC2/HBase IP addresses tied to this specific course submission. Those have been replaced with placeholders (`<REDACTED-grading-secret>`, `<producer-ip>`, etc.) before publishing here.

## Note on what's excluded

The original zip included an AWS Lambda deployment package containing ~270 vendored third-party Python library files (`requests`, `urllib3`, `idna`, `certifi`, `charset_normalizer`, plus compiled `.pyd` binaries and `__pycache__` files) needed to run the Lambda function in AWS's environment. Those are pip dependencies, not original code, so they're left out of this repo — `traffic_generator_lambda.py` above is the actual handler code from that package.

## Tech stack

Python · Flask · Apache Kafka · AWS MSK (Managed Streaming for Kafka) · AWS Lambda · AWS EC2 · Amazon EMR · HBase · Prometheus · Grafana · boto3
